mcqs.question: Which memory management technique divides memory into fixed-sized blocks called frames?
mcqs.explanation: Paging divides both virtual and physical memory into fixed-sized blocks. Virtual memory blocks are called pages and physical memory blocks are called frames.

mcqs.question: In which type of fragmentation does wasted space occur within allocated memory blocks?
mcqs.explanation: Internal fragmentation occurs when memory allocated is larger than requested, leaving unused space inside the allocated block.

mcqs.question: Which page replacement algorithm always replaces the oldest page in memory?
mcqs.explanation: FIFO (First In First Out) replaces the page that has been in memory the longest, regardless of how recently it was accessed.

mcqs.question: What is the primary function of the Memory Management Unit (MMU)?

options.text: Translating virtual addresses to physical addresses

mcqs.explanation: The MMU is hardware that translates virtual (logical) addresses generated by the CPU into physical addresses in RAM.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid page replacement algorithm?
mcqs.explanation: SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First) is a disk scheduling algorithm, not a page replacement algorithm.

mcqs.question: What happens during a page fault?

options.text: The requested page is not in memory and must be loaded from disk

mcqs.explanation: A page fault occurs when a process tries to access a page that is not currently in physical memory, requiring the OS to load it from secondary storage.

mcqs.question: Which technique combines the advantages of paging and segmentation?
mcqs.explanation: Segmented paging divides each segment into pages, combining protection benefits of segmentation with memory efficiency of paging.

mcqs.question: What is thrashing caused by?

options.text: Insufficient memory causing excessive page swapping

mcqs.explanation: Thrashing occurs when the system spends more time swapping pages in and out than executing processes due to insufficient physical memory.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is an advantage of virtual memory?

options.text: Allows execution of programs larger than physical memory
Eliminates the need for secondary storage
Removes the need for memory protection

mcqs.explanation: Virtual memory allows programs to use more memory than physically available by paging portions to disk, enabling execution of larger programs.

mcqs.question: In demand paging, when are pages loaded into memory?
mcqs.explanation: Demand paging loads pages into memory only when they are referenced by a running process, deferring loading until actually needed.

mcqs.question: What is the main drawback of the FIFO page replacement algorithm?

options.text: It is too complex to implement
It may suffer from Belady's anomaly (more frames can cause more page faults)

mcqs.explanation: Belady's anomaly is the phenomenon where FIFO can result in more page faults when using more memory frames, which is counterintuitive.

mcqs.question: What type of address translation does paging use?
mcqs.explanation: Paging uses fixed-size blocks (pages and frames) for address translation, dividing both virtual and physical memory into uniform blocks.

mcqs.question: Which memory management technique requires memory to be contiguous?
mcqs.explanation: Contiguous memory allocation requires each process to be allocated a single continuous block of memory.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of a Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)?

options.text: To cache recent address translations for fast access

mcqs.explanation: TLB is a hardware cache that stores recent virtual-to-physical address translations to speed up memory access.

mcqs.question: Which optimal page replacement algorithm makes the best decision about which page to replace?

options.text: Replaces the page used farthest in the future
Replaces the page used most recently

mcqs.explanation: The Optimal algorithm replaces the page that will not be used for the longest time in the future, which is theoretically best but not implementable in practice.

mcqs.question: What is the relationship between pages and frames in paging?
mcqs.explanation: In paging, pages and frames are the same size. Pages are fixed-sized blocks of virtual memory and frames are same-sized blocks of physical memory.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a disadvantage of segmentation?
mcqs.explanation: Segmentation can suffer from external fragmentation because segments are variable-sized and can leave holes in memory.

mcqs.question: What is the working set of a process?

options.text: The set of pages frequently accessed over a time interval
The total virtual memory of a process
The stack memory of a process

mcqs.explanation: The working set is the set of pages a process has referenced during a recent time interval, representing the memory needed for effective execution.

mcqs.question: Which page replacement algorithm uses reference bits and considers both age and usage?
mcqs.explanation: Second Chance (Clock) algorithm uses a reference bit to give pages a second chance if recently used, combining aspects of FIFO with usage tracking.

mcqs.question: What is copy-on-write used for in memory management?

options.text: To efficiently share memory between processes until modification

mcqs.explanation: Copy-on-write allows processes to share memory pages initially. When a process modifies a page, a separate copy is created for that process.
